class Rename(Tensor): 
796class Rename(Tensor):
797    """
798    A tensor that renames the edges of an inner tensor.
799
800    This is used to change free edge names (and sometimes inner edge names)
801    without modifying the underlying contraction.
802    """
803
804    def __init__(self, inner: Tensor, rename: dict[str, str]):
805        """
806        Initialize a Rename tensor.
807
808        Args:
809            inner: The tensor to be renamed.
810            rename: A dictionary mapping old edge names to new names.
811        """
812        self.name = "Rename"
813        self.tensor = inner
814        self.mapping = {k: v for k, v in rename.items() if k != v}
815        self._shape = {rename.get(e, e): s for e, s in inner.shape.items()}
816
817    def __repr__(self) -> str:
818        argstring = ", ".join(f'{k}="{v}"' for k, v in self.mapping.items())
819        return f"{self.tensor}.rename({argstring})"
820
821    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
822        return self.tensor.depends_on(x)
823
824    def _grad(self, x: "Variable", new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
825        # If the new names are used in inner, we need to rename them, and then add the
826        # new names to our own rename dict.
827        middle = _unused_edge_names(new_names.values(), self.edges | self.tensor.edges)
828        middle_names = {o: middle[n] for o, n in new_names.items()}
829        middle_to_new = {middle[n]: n for o, n in new_names.items()}
830        return Rename(self.tensor.grad(x, middle_names), middle_to_new | self.mapping)
831
832    @classmethod
833    def merge_renames(cls, *renames: dict[str, str]) -> dict[str, str]:
834        """
835        Merge several renaming dictionaries into one.
836
837        Args:
838            *renames: Arbitrary number of renaming dictionaries.
839
840        Returns:
841            A single dictionary representing the merged renaming.
842        """
843        merged: dict[str, str] = {}
844        for rename in renames:
845            used = merged.keys() | merged.values()
846            # Apply the rename to the existing chains
847            for o, n in merged.items():
848                merged[o] = rename.get(n, n)
849            # Add new renames
850            for o, n in rename.items():
851                if o not in used:
852                    merged[o] = n
853        return merged
854
855    def _simplify(self, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
856        inner = self.tensor.simplify(args)
857        if isinstance(inner, Rename):
858            merged = self.merge_renames(inner.mapping, self.mapping)
859            res = inner.tensor.rename(**merged)
860        else:
861            res = inner.rename(**self.mapping)
862        while isinstance(res, Rename) and not res.mapping:
863            res = res.tensor
864        return res
865
866    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
867        return Rename(self.tensor, self.merge_renames(self.mapping, kwargs))
868
869    def _substitute(self, x: "Variable", y: "Tensor", memo: dict) -> "Tensor":
870        inner = _substitute_memo(self.tensor, x, y, memo)
871        return self if inner is self.tensor else Rename(inner, self.mapping)
872
873    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
874        # Rename is the only tensor that doesn't actually create its own node in the graph
875        # This allows Variable(i, j=i).with_symmetries("i j") to be isomorphic to
876        # Rename(Variable(j, i=j).with_symmetries("i j"), {"i": "j", "j": "i"})
877        G, t_edges = self.tensor.structural_graph()
878        edges = {}
879        for o, node in t_edges.items():
880            e = self.mapping.get(o, o)
881            edges[e] = node
882        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
883        return G, edges

A tensor that renames the edges of an inner tensor.

This is used to change free edge names (and sometimes inner edge names) without modifying the underlying contraction.

Rename(inner: Tensor, rename: dict[str, str]) 
804    def __init__(self, inner: Tensor, rename: dict[str, str]):
805        """
806        Initialize a Rename tensor.
807
808        Args:
809            inner: The tensor to be renamed.
810            rename: A dictionary mapping old edge names to new names.
811        """
812        self.name = "Rename"
813        self.tensor = inner
814        self.mapping = {k: v for k, v in rename.items() if k != v}
815        self._shape = {rename.get(e, e): s for e, s in inner.shape.items()}

Initialize a Rename tensor.

Args: inner: The tensor to be renamed. rename: A dictionary mapping old edge names to new names.

name
tensor
mapping
def depends_on(self, x: Variable) -> bool: 
821    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
822        return self.tensor.depends_on(x)

Check if this tensor depends on the variable x.

@classmethod
def merge_renames(cls, *renames: dict[str, str]) -> dict[str, str]: 
832    @classmethod
833    def merge_renames(cls, *renames: dict[str, str]) -> dict[str, str]:
834        """
835        Merge several renaming dictionaries into one.
836
837        Args:
838            *renames: Arbitrary number of renaming dictionaries.
839
840        Returns:
841            A single dictionary representing the merged renaming.
842        """
843        merged: dict[str, str] = {}
844        for rename in renames:
845            used = merged.keys() | merged.values()
846            # Apply the rename to the existing chains
847            for o, n in merged.items():
848                merged[o] = rename.get(n, n)
849            # Add new renames
850            for o, n in rename.items():
851                if o not in used:
852                    merged[o] = n
853        return merged

Merge several renaming dictionaries into one.

Args: *renames: Arbitrary number of renaming dictionaries.

Returns: A single dictionary representing the merged renaming.

def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
873    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
874        # Rename is the only tensor that doesn't actually create its own node in the graph
875        # This allows Variable(i, j=i).with_symmetries("i j") to be isomorphic to
876        # Rename(Variable(j, i=j).with_symmetries("i j"), {"i": "j", "j": "i"})
877        G, t_edges = self.tensor.structural_graph()
878        edges = {}
879        for o, node in t_edges.items():
880            e = self.mapping.get(o, o)
881            edges[e] = node
882        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
883        return G, edges

Create a graph representation of the tensor, which can be used for isomorphism testing.

Returns: A tuple with the following values: - A NetworkX directed graph. - Each node in the top tree is labeled with the producing tensor. - Use name=hashable to label vertices - "edges", a dict of edge_name -> node id - Node 0 should be the root

class Constant(Tensor, abc.ABC): 
886class Constant(Tensor, ABC):
887    """
888    An abstract tensor that represents a constant (non-variable) tensor.
889    """
890
891    def __init__(self, *shape0: Symbol, _symmetries: Optional[set[frozenset[str]]] = None, **shape1: Symbol):
892        """
893        A constant tensor with the given edges.
894
895        Args:
896            shape0: Positional dimensions (as Sympy symbols).
897            _symmetries: Optional symmetry groups.
898            shape1: Keyword dimensions.
899        """
900        self.name = "NotImplemented"
901        self._shape = self._check_shape(shape0, shape1)
902        self._symmetries = self._check_symmetries(self._shape, _symmetries)
903
904    def __repr__(self) -> str:
905        return f"{type(self).__name__}({self.shape})"
906
907    def with_symmetries(self, symmetries: str | set[frozenset[str]]) -> "Constant":
908        return type(self)(self.name, **self._shape, _symmetries=symmetries)
909
910    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
911        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
912        G.add_node(0, name=type(self).__name__, tensor=self)
913        edges = {}
914        for size in set(self.shape.values()):
915            # All of this is more or less equal to Variable
916            G.add_node(size_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name=f"size={size.name}({id(size)})")
917            G.add_edge(0, size_node)
918            # Find each orbit with the given size (see note above about fine-grained-ness)
919            for orbit in self._symmetries:
920                e, *_ = orbit
921                if self.shape[e] != size:
922                    continue
923                G.add_node(orbit_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name="Orbit Node")
924                G.add_edge(size_node, orbit_node)
925                for e in orbit:
926                    edges[e] = orbit_node
927        return G, edges
928
929    def _substitute(self, x: "Variable", y: "Tensor", memo: dict) -> "Tensor":
930        return self
931
932    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
933        return type(self)(**{kwargs.get(e, e): s for e, s in self.shape.items()})
934
935    def _grad(self, x: Variable, new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
936        return Zero(**(self.shape | {new_names[e]: s for e, s in x.shape.items()}))
937
938    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
939        return False

An abstract tensor that represents a constant (non-variable) tensor.

Constant( *shape0: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol, _symmetries: Optional[set[frozenset[str]]] = None, **shape1: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol) 
891    def __init__(self, *shape0: Symbol, _symmetries: Optional[set[frozenset[str]]] = None, **shape1: Symbol):
892        """
893        A constant tensor with the given edges.
894
895        Args:
896            shape0: Positional dimensions (as Sympy symbols).
897            _symmetries: Optional symmetry groups.
898            shape1: Keyword dimensions.
899        """
900        self.name = "NotImplemented"
901        self._shape = self._check_shape(shape0, shape1)
902        self._symmetries = self._check_symmetries(self._shape, _symmetries)

908        return type(self)(self.name, **self._shape, _symmetries=symmetries)
def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
910    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
911        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
912        G.add_node(0, name=type(self).__name__, tensor=self)
913        edges = {}
914        for size in set(self.shape.values()):
915            # All of this is more or less equal to Variable
916            G.add_node(size_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name=f"size={size.name}({id(size)})")
917            G.add_edge(0, size_node)
918            # Find each orbit with the given size (see note above about fine-grained-ness)
919            for orbit in self._symmetries:
920                e, *_ = orbit
921                if self.shape[e] != size:
922                    continue
923                G.add_node(orbit_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name="Orbit Node")
924                G.add_edge(size_node, orbit_node)
925                for e in orbit:
926                    edges[e] = orbit_node
927        return G, edges

class Delta(Constant): 
 942class Delta(Constant):
 943    """The "Delta" tensor is defined by C_{i,j,k} = 1 if i == j == k, else 0
 944    Or alternatively as Δₙ = ∑ₓ (eₓ)⊗ⁿ, where are e_i are basis vectors
 945    For order 2, this is the identity matrix.
 946        Note: For higher order, the identity tensor is the product of n identity matrices,
 947        (order 2 Delta's), not the order-n Delta tensor.
 948    For order 0, the Delta tensor is defined as |n|, where n is the size/dimension of the tensor.
 949    """
 950
 951    def __init__(self, size: Symbol, *edges: str):
 952        """
 953        Initialize a Delta tensor.
 954
 955        Args:
 956            size: The size (dimension) of the tensor.
 957            edges: The names of the tensor edges.
 958        """
 959        edges = self._check_edges(edges)
 960        super().__init__(**{e: size for e in edges})
 961        assert isinstance(size, (Symbol, Number)), "Size must be a sympy symbol"
 962        self._size = size
 963
 964    @property
 965    def size(self) -> Symbol:
 966        return self._size
 967
 968    def __repr__(self) -> str:
 969        if not self.edges:
 970            return f"Delta({self.size})"
 971        return f"Delta({self.size}, \"{', '.join(self.edges)}\")"
 972
 973    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
 974        return Delta(self.size, *[kwargs.get(e, e) for e in self.edges])
 975
 976    @classmethod
 977    def simplify_outer(cls, tensors: list[Tensor]) -> list[Tensor]:
 978        """Simplifies a list of tensors assumed to be a product."""
 979        while True:
 980            tensors, done = cls._simplify_step(tensors)
 981            if done:
 982                break
 983        return tensors
 984
 985    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
 986        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
 987        G.add_node(0, name=type(self).__name__, tensor=self)
 988        G.add_node(size_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name=f"size={self.size.name}({id(self.size)})")
 989        G.add_edge(0, size_node)
 990        return G, {e: size_node for e in self.edges}
 991
 992    ################################################################################
 993    # There are many simplify rules for Delta. We split them into separate methods for clarity.
 994
 995    @classmethod
 996    def _simplify_step(cls, tensors: list[Tensor]) -> tuple[list[Tensor], bool]:
 997        """Performs one step of simplification. Returns a new list if changed, or the original if not."""
 998        for e, ts in _group_edges(tensors).items():
 999            if len(ts) == 1:
1000                continue
1001            t1, t2 = ts
1002            assert t1.shape[e] == t2.shape[e], f"{t1.shape[e]=} != {t2.shape[e]}, {e=}"
1003
1004            # Remove t1 and t2 from tensors.  We use the "is" operator, since equality
1005            # means isomorphic, so it might remove too many unintended tensors.
1006            other = [t for t in tensors if t is not t1 and t is not t2]
1007
1008            for simplification in [
1009                cls._merge_copy_tensors,
1010                cls._remove_identity_matrix,
1011                cls._sum_constrained_variable,
1012            ]:
1013                if (new := simplification(t1, t2, e)) is not None:
1014                    return other + new, False
1015
1016        return tensors, True
1017
1018    @staticmethod
1019    def _merge_copy_tensors(t1: Tensor, t2: Tensor, e: str) -> Optional[list[Tensor]]:
1020        if not (isinstance(t1, Delta) and isinstance(t2, Delta)):
1021            return None
1022
1023        # We can't merge order 0 copy tensors, since we now give them a value equal to their size.
1024        # In principle we could create a new Delta(size1 * size2, []) tensor, but then we start having
1025        # arbitrary expressions as sizes, which I'm not sure we want yet.
1026        # Also, merging an order 0 with an order > 0 was never going to work, unless the size of the
1027        # order 0 Delta was 1, which it probably never is.
1028        # In either case, this method should only be called if the tensors share an edge, which they
1029        # can't if one of them has order 0.
1030        assert t1.order != 0 and t2.order != 0, "Can't merge order 0 Delta tensors"
1031
1032        # Since the tensors are connected, we can assume they have the same size
1033        assert t1.size == t2.size, "Contracted Delta tensors must have same size"
1034        size = t1.size
1035
1036        # We don't just remove e, but remove all shared edges
1037        # The amazing thing is that even in the case where all edges disappear, we
1038        # still get to keep information on the "size" of the Delta tensor.
1039        return [Delta(size, *(t1.edges ^ t2.edges))]
1040
1041    @staticmethod
1042    def _remove_identity_matrix(t1: Tensor, t2: Tensor, e: str) -> Optional[list[Tensor]]:
1043        # If both are Delta's, we use the previous method
1044        if isinstance(t1, Delta) and isinstance(t2, Delta):
1045            return None
1046
1047        # Make t1 the identity matrix
1048        if isinstance(t2, Delta) and t2.order == 2:
1049            t1, t2 = t2, t1
1050
1051        if isinstance(t1, Delta) and t1.order == 2:
1052            # Find the edge of t1 that's not e
1053            other_edge = next(iter(set(t1.edges) - {e}))
1054            # Don't create self loops. We never connect a tensor to itself.
1055            # Unless it's another Delta, in which case we already handled it above.
1056            if other_edge not in t2.edges:
1057                return [t2.rename(**{e: other_edge})]
1058        return None
1059
1060    @staticmethod
1061    def _sum_constrained_variable(t1: Tensor, t2: Tensor, e: str) -> Optional[list[Tensor]]:
1062        """Sum over a simplex-constrained edge of a Variable.
1063
1064        Contracting an edge with an order-1 Delta (an all-ones vector; the pattern
1065        ``F.sum`` produces) computes the sum over that edge. If the edge is declared
1066        ``simplex`` on the variable (see ``Variable.with_constraint``), that sum is 1,
1067        so the variable and the ones-vector are replaced by all-ones on the variable's
1068        remaining edges.
1069        """
1070        # Make t1 the all-ones vector
1071        if not (isinstance(t1, Delta) and t1.order == 1):
1072            t1, t2 = t2, t1
1073        if not (isinstance(t1, Delta) and t1.order == 1):
1074            return None
1075        # Peel lazy Rename wrappers off t2 to find the underlying Variable, tracking the
1076        # accumulated original -> outer name mapping.
1077        inner, mapping = t2, {}
1078        while isinstance(inner, Rename):
1079            mapping = Rename.merge_renames(inner.mapping, mapping)
1080            inner = inner.tensor
1081        if not isinstance(inner, Variable):
1082            return None
1083        # Map the contracted edge back to the variable's original edge name, since
1084        # constraints are declared in terms of the original names.
1085        orig_e = {mapping.get(o, o): o for o in inner.edges}.get(e)
1086        if ("simplex", orig_e) not in inner._constraints:
1087            return None
1088        return [Delta(s, e2) for e2, s in t2.shape.items() if e2 != e]

The "Delta" tensor is defined by C_{i,j,k} = 1 if i == j == k, else 0 Or alternatively as Δₙ = ∑ₓ (eₓ)⊗ⁿ, where are e_i are basis vectors For order 2, this is the identity matrix. Note: For higher order, the identity tensor is the product of n identity matrices, (order 2 Delta's), not the order-n Delta tensor. For order 0, the Delta tensor is defined as |n|, where n is the size/dimension of the tensor.

Delta(size: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol, *edges: str) 
951    def __init__(self, size: Symbol, *edges: str):
952        """
953        Initialize a Delta tensor.
954
955        Args:
956            size: The size (dimension) of the tensor.
957            edges: The names of the tensor edges.
958        """
959        edges = self._check_edges(edges)
960        super().__init__(**{e: size for e in edges})
961        assert isinstance(size, (Symbol, Number)), "Size must be a sympy symbol"
962        self._size = size

Args: size: The size (dimension) of the tensor. edges: The names of the tensor edges.

size: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol

965    def size(self) -> Symbol:
966        return self._size
@classmethod
def simplify_outer( cls, tensors: list[Tensor]) -> list[Tensor]: 
976    @classmethod
977    def simplify_outer(cls, tensors: list[Tensor]) -> list[Tensor]:
978        """Simplifies a list of tensors assumed to be a product."""
979        while True:
980            tensors, done = cls._simplify_step(tensors)
981            if done:
982                break
983        return tensors

def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
985    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
986        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
987        G.add_node(0, name=type(self).__name__, tensor=self)
988        G.add_node(size_node := G.number_of_nodes(), name=f"size={self.size.name}({id(self.size)})")
989        G.add_edge(0, size_node)
990        return G, {e: size_node for e in self.edges}

def Ones( *shape0: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol, **shape1: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol) -> Tensor: 
1095def Ones(*shape0: Symbol, **shape1: Symbol) -> Tensor:
1096    """Matrix such that O_{i,j,k} = 1 for all i, j, k"""
1097    # Implemented in practice by a simple outer product of Delta's
1098    shape = Tensor._check_shape(shape0, shape1)
1099    return Product([Delta(s, e) for e, s in shape.items()])

Matrix such that O_{i,j,k} = 1 for all i, j, k

@dataclass
class FunctionSignature(abc.ABC): 
1107@dataclass
1108class FunctionSignature(ABC):
1109    """
1110    An abstract base class representing a function that takes one or more tensors as input
1111    and produces a new tensor as output.
1112
1113    This class is designed to capture the symbolic structure of a tensor operation by
1114    listing its output edges (`edges`) and the input edges (`inputs`) each of its input
1115    tensors must provide. Subclasses are required to implement the `eval` and `derivative`
1116    methods, while `simplify` has a default no-op implementation that may be overridden.
1117
1118    Parameters
1119    ----------
1120    name : str
1121        A descriptive name for the function (e.g., "matmul", "unfold", "ce", "attention").
1122    edges : Set[str]
1123        The output edges of this function. These are string identifiers that symbolically
1124        represent the dimensions of the output tensor.
1125    inputs : tuple[Set[str], ...]
1126        A tuple where each element is a set of edges required from one input tensor.
1127        For example, if the first input tensor must share dimension edges {"b", "i"} and
1128        the second input tensor must share dimension edges {"b", "j"}, then
1129        `inputs = ({"b", "i"}, {"b", "j"})`.
1130
1131    Examples
1132    --------
1133    1) **Matrix multiplication**:
1134       Suppose `x` has shape (b, i, j) and `y` has shape (b, j, k). We might define:
1135
1136       .. code-block:: python
1137
1138          f = MatmulSignature(
1139              name="matmul",
1140              edges={"i", "k"},
1141              inputs=({"i", "j"}, {"j", "k"})
1142          )
1143
1144       Here, the function consumes edges `i` and `j` from `x` and edges `j` and `k` from `y`.
1145       The edge `b` is shared between the two inputs and broadcasted. The output tensor
1146       eventually has shape (b, i, k).
1147
1148    2) **Unfold**:
1149       Suppose we have a tensor `x` with shape (b, c, w, h) and we want to produce a series
1150       of patches. We might define:
1151
1152       .. code-block:: python
1153
1154          f = UnfoldSignature(
1155              name="unfold",
1156              edges={"patch", "c_out", "w_out", "h_out"},
1157              inputs=({"c_in", "w_in", "h_in"},)
1158          )
1159
1160       This function consumes edges ``c, w, h`` from `x` and introduces a new edge
1161       named ``patch`` of size (w_in - w_out + 1)*(h_in - h_out + 1).
1162
1163    3) **Cross-entropy**:
1164       This is an example of a function that takes two inputs, for example logits and
1165       probabilities. Suppose:
1166       - `x` has shape (b, logits)
1167       - `y` has shape (b, probs)
1168
1169       We might define:
1170
1171       .. code-block:: python
1172
1173          f = CrossEntropySignature(
1174              name="ce",
1175              edges={"ce"},
1176              inputs=({"logits"}, {"probs"})
1177          )
1178
1179       Both inputs share the batch edge ``b``. The new edge ``ce`` could represent the
1180       cross-entropy output dimension (often scalar per example, so shape (b, ce)).
1181
1182    4) **Multi-query Dot Product Attention**:
1183       Suppose we have tensors:
1184       - `q` with shape (b, n_q, d)
1185       - `k` with shape (b, seq, d)
1186       - `v` with shape (b, seq, d_out)
1187
1188       Conceptually, to compute multi-query attention:
1189
1190       - We form (b, n_q, seq) via a dot product of `q` and `k`.
1191       - We apply a softmax to get (b, n_q, prob).
1192       - We multiply by `v` to get (b, n_q, d_out).
1193
1194       A possible signature might be:
1195
1196       .. code-block:: python
1197
1198          f = AttentionSignature(
1199              name="attention",
1200              edges={"n_q", "d_out"},
1201              inputs=(
1202                  {"n_q", "d"},
1203                  {"seq", "d"},
1204                  {"seq", "d_out"},
1205              )
1206          )
1207
1208       The edge ``b`` is shared among all inputs (broadcast). The final output
1209       has shape (b, n_q, d_out).
1210    """
1211
1212    name: str
1213    # The output edges of the function. Note no Symbols here, since we don't know the sizes yet.
1214    edges: set[str]
1215    # Defines the number of input tensors the function takes, and what edges it needs from each
1216    inputs: tuple[set[str], ...]
1217
1218    # TODO: Functions should support symmetric outputs, just like Constant and Variable.
1219    # Actually we could define symmetries for the inputs too, since the order doesn't always matter.
1220
1221    def derivative(self, i: int, new_edges: dict[str, str] = None) -> "FunctionSignature":
1222        """
1223        Returns a new FunctionSignature that represents the derivative
1224        with respect to the i-th input, and where edges are created
1225        according to `new_edges` a dict {old_name : new_name}.
1226        """
1227        if new_edges is None:
1228            new_edges = {}
1229        if self.inputs[i] != new_edges.keys():
1230            raise ValueError(f"Expected {self.inputs[i]} but got {new_edges.keys()}")
1231        if self.edges & set(new_edges.values()):
1232            raise ValueError(f"New edges {new_edges.values()} clash with {self.edges=}")
1233        return FunctionSignature(f"D_{i}{self.name}", self.edges | set(new_edges.values()), self.inputs)
1234
1235    def simplify(self, t: "Function", args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1236        """
1237        Simplifies the Function tensor that uses this signature.
1238        Default implementation returns `t` unchanged.
1239        Subclasses can override this method for custom simplifications.
1240        """
1241        return t
1242
1243    def __repr__(self) -> str:
1244        return f"FunctionSignature('{self.name}', {set(self.edges)}, {[set(s) for s in self.inputs]})"

An abstract base class representing a function that takes one or more tensors as input and produces a new tensor as output.

This class is designed to capture the symbolic structure of a tensor operation by listing its output edges (edges) and the input edges (inputs) each of its input tensors must provide. Subclasses are required to implement the eval and derivative methods, while simplify has a default no-op implementation that may be overridden.

Parameters

name : str A descriptive name for the function (e.g., "matmul", "unfold", "ce", "attention"). edges : Set[str] The output edges of this function. These are string identifiers that symbolically represent the dimensions of the output tensor. inputs : tuple[Set[str], ...] A tuple where each element is a set of edges required from one input tensor. For example, if the first input tensor must share dimension edges {"b", "i"} and the second input tensor must share dimension edges {"b", "j"}, then inputs = ({"b", "i"}, {"b", "j"}).

Examples

1) Matrix multiplication: Suppose x has shape (b, i, j) and y has shape (b, j, k). We might define:

```python f = MatmulSignature( name="matmul", edges={"i", "k"}, inputs=({"i", "j"}, {"j", "k"}) )


   Here, the function consumes edges `i` and `j` from `x` and edges `j` and `k` from `y`.
   The edge `b` is shared between the two inputs and broadcasted. The output tensor
   eventually has shape (b, i, k).

2) **Unfold**:
   Suppose we have a tensor `x` with shape (b, c, w, h) and we want to produce a series
   of patches. We might define:

   ```python
f = UnfoldSignature(
    name="unfold",
    edges={"patch", "c_out", "w_out", "h_out"},
    inputs=({"c_in", "w_in", "h_in"},)
)

This function consumes edges c, w, h from x and introduces a new edge named patch of size (w_in - w_out + 1)*(h_in - h_out + 1).

3) Cross-entropy: This is an example of a function that takes two inputs, for example logits and probabilities. Suppose:

  • x has shape (b, logits)
  • y has shape (b, probs)

We might define:

```python f = CrossEntropySignature( name="ce", edges={"ce"}, inputs=({"logits"}, {"probs"}) )


   Both inputs share the batch edge ``b``. The new edge ``ce`` could represent the
   cross-entropy output dimension (often scalar per example, so shape (b, ce)).

4) **Multi-query Dot Product Attention**:
   Suppose we have tensors:
   - `q` with shape (b, n_q, d)
   - `k` with shape (b, seq, d)
   - `v` with shape (b, seq, d_out)

   Conceptually, to compute multi-query attention:

   - We form (b, n_q, seq) via a dot product of `q` and `k`.
   - We apply a softmax to get (b, n_q, prob).
   - We multiply by `v` to get (b, n_q, d_out).

   A possible signature might be:

   ```python
f = AttentionSignature(
    name="attention",
    edges={"n_q", "d_out"},
    inputs=(
        {"n_q", "d"},
        {"seq", "d"},
        {"seq", "d_out"},
    )
)

The edge b is shared among all inputs (broadcast). The final output has shape (b, n_q, d_out).

FunctionSignature(name: str, edges: set[str], inputs: tuple[set[str], ...])
name: str
edges: set[str]
inputs: tuple[set[str], ...]
def derivative( self, i: int, new_edges: dict[str, str] = None) -> FunctionSignature: 
1221    def derivative(self, i: int, new_edges: dict[str, str] = None) -> "FunctionSignature":
1222        """
1223        Returns a new FunctionSignature that represents the derivative
1224        with respect to the i-th input, and where edges are created
1225        according to `new_edges` a dict {old_name : new_name}.
1226        """
1227        if new_edges is None:
1228            new_edges = {}
1229        if self.inputs[i] != new_edges.keys():
1230            raise ValueError(f"Expected {self.inputs[i]} but got {new_edges.keys()}")
1231        if self.edges & set(new_edges.values()):
1232            raise ValueError(f"New edges {new_edges.values()} clash with {self.edges=}")
1233        return FunctionSignature(f"D_{i}{self.name}", self.edges | set(new_edges.values()), self.inputs)

Returns a new FunctionSignature that represents the derivative with respect to the i-th input, and where edges are created according to new_edges a dict {old_name : new_name}.

def simplify( self, t: Function, args: dict[str, typing.Any]) -> Tensor: 
1235    def simplify(self, t: "Function", args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1236        """
1237        Simplifies the Function tensor that uses this signature.
1238        Default implementation returns `t` unchanged.
1239        Subclasses can override this method for custom simplifications.
1240        """
1241        return t

Simplifies the Function tensor that uses this signature. Default implementation returns t unchanged. Subclasses can override this method for custom simplifications.

def function( name: str, output_shape: dict[str, sympy.core.symbol.Symbol], *inputs: tuple[Tensor | str, ...]) -> Function: 
1247def function(
1248    name: str,
1249    output_shape: dict[str, Symbol],
1250    *inputs: tuple[Tensor | str, ...],
1251) -> "Function":
1252    """
1253    Create a Function tensor from a name, output shape and inputs.
1254
1255    Args:
1256        name: The function name.
1257        output_shape: A dictionary mapping output edge names to sizes.
1258        inputs: A sequence of tuples where each tuple starts with a tensor (or string)
1259                followed by the required edge names.
1260
1261    Returns:
1262        A Function tensor.
1263    """
1264    return Function(
1265        FunctionSignature(
1266            name,
1267            output_shape.keys(),
1268            [set(es) for (t, *es) in inputs],
1269        ),
1270        [t for (t, *es) in inputs],
1271        output_shape,
1272    )

Create a Function tensor from a name, output shape and inputs.

Args: name: The function name. output_shape: A dictionary mapping output edge names to sizes. inputs: A sequence of tuples where each tuple starts with a tensor (or string) followed by the required edge names.

Returns: A Function tensor.

class Function(Tensor): 
1275class Function(Tensor):
1276    """
1277    A tensor that represents a function applied to one or more input tensors.
1278
1279    Attributes:
1280        signature: The FunctionSignature for this function.
1281        inputs: A list of input tensors.
1282        shape_out: A dictionary mapping output edge names to sizes.
1283    """
1284
1285    def __init__(
1286        self,
1287        signature: FunctionSignature,
1288        inputs: Iterable[Tensor],
1289        shape_out: dict[str, Symbol],
1290    ):
1291        """
1292        A function tensor that takes one or more input tensors and produces an output tensor.
1293
1294        Args:
1295            signature: The FunctionSignature defining this function, or a string giving the function name.
1296            inputs: The input tensors and their input edge names. [(t0, e00, e01, ...), (t1, e10, ...), ...]
1297            shape_out: The names of the output edges of this function.
1298        """
1299        self.shape_out = self._check_shape([], shape_out)
1300        self.inputs = list(inputs)
1301        self.signature = signature
1302
1303        # Validation
1304        if self.shape_out.keys() != signature.edges:
1305            raise ValueError(f"Signature doesn't match shape: {self.shape_out.keys()=} != {signature.edges}")
1306        if len(self.inputs) != len(signature.inputs):
1307            raise ValueError(f"Expected {len(signature.inputs)} inputs, got {len(self.inputs)}")
1308        for t, es in zip(self.inputs, signature.inputs):
1309            if es - t.edges:
1310                raise ValueError(f"Input edges {es} not subset of {t.edges=}")
1311
1312        # Build final shape = union of output edges + broadcast edges from each input
1313        # Note that this approach pushes all the broadcasted edges to the end of the shape.
1314        # But we don't care about the order of the edges, so it's fine.
1315        self._shape = dict(self.shape_out)
1316        # We allow "broadcasting" for edges that are not in fn_sig.inputs[i].
1317        # So if an input has edges that are not used, they're broadcasted.
1318        for t, es in zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs):
1319            # any edges in t.edges - es are "broadcast"
1320            broadcast_edges = t.edges - es
1321            if broadcast_edges & self._shape.keys():
1322                raise ValueError("Broadcast edges must not be shared between inputs")
1323            for b in broadcast_edges:
1324                self._shape[b] = t.shape[b]
1325
1326    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
1327        # Names to avoid when generating fresh middle names below.
1328        used = self.edges | set(kwargs.values())
1329        for t in self.inputs:
1330            used |= t.edges
1331        renamed_inputs = []
1332        final_rename = {}  # applied on top of the rebuilt Function at the end
1333        for t, es in zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs):
1334            # Only rename external (broadcast) edges of input tensors.
1335            rename = {e: v for e, v in kwargs.items() if e in t.edges and e not in es}
1336            # A rename target may collide with an edge of t that we are not renaming,
1337            # typically an edge consumed by the function (e.g. renaming broadcast edge
1338            # 's' -> 't' while the function consumes t's edge 't'). Route such renames
1339            # through fresh middle names and fix them up with a final Rename,
1340            # mirroring the middle-name routing in Rename._grad.
1341            remaining = t.edges - rename.keys()
1342            colliding = {e: v for e, v in rename.items() if v in remaining}
1343            if colliding:
1344                middle = _unused_edge_names(colliding.values(), used)
1345                used |= set(middle.values())
1346                for e, v in colliding.items():
1347                    rename[e] = middle[v]
1348                    final_rename[middle[v]] = v
1349            renamed_inputs.append(t.rename(**rename))
1350        output_rename = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items() if k in self.shape_out}
1351        final_rename |= output_rename
1352        res = Function(
1353            self.signature,
1354            renamed_inputs,
1355            self.shape_out,
1356        )
1357        if any(v != k for k, v in final_rename.items()):
1358            res = Rename(res, final_rename)
1359        return res
1360
1361    def _simplify(self, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1362        new_inputs = [t.simplify(args=args) for t in self.inputs]
1363
1364        # Broadcasting can be pulled out of the function.
1365        pulled_out = []
1366        new_inputs2 = []
1367        for t, es in zip(new_inputs, self.signature.inputs):
1368            if isinstance(t, Product):
1369                new_prod = []
1370                for u in t.factors:
1371                    if (
1372                        isinstance(u, Delta)
1373                        and u.order == 1
1374                        and list(u.edges)[0] in t.edges
1375                        and list(u.edges)[0] not in es
1376                    ):
1377                        pulled_out.append(u)
1378                    else:
1379                        new_prod.append(u)
1380                new_inputs2.append(Product(new_prod))
1381            else:
1382                new_inputs2.append(t)
1383        new_inputs = new_inputs2
1384
1385        res = Function(self.signature, new_inputs, self.shape_out)
1386
1387        # This results in an extra simplify call to all the children of the function.
1388        # Maybe we can avoid this somehow?
1389        old_shape = res.shape
1390        res = self.signature.simplify(res, args)
1391        assert res.shape == old_shape, "Function signature simplify should not change shape"
1392
1393        if pulled_out:
1394            res = Product([res] + pulled_out)
1395
1396        return res
1397
1398    def _grad(self, x: Variable, new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
1399        # We sum over each input function, just like the normal chain rule:
1400        # d/dx f(g₁(x), …, gₖ(x)) = Σᵢ₌₁ᵏ (d/dx gᵢ(x)) Dᵢf(g₁(x), …, gₖ(x))
1401
1402        # D_i adds a new output edge to the function, which is contracted with
1403        # the normal output edge of the tensor. So we need to make sure this doesn't
1404        # clash with an existing output edge of f.
1405        parts = []
1406        for i, (t, input_edges) in enumerate(zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs)):
1407            # Take the derivative of the outer function
1408            # We need "connection" edges for each edge in input_edges. Mostly we could just use the same name
1409            # but they need to avoid clashing with "new_names" and the output edges of the tensor.
1410            connection_names = _unused_edge_names(input_edges, self.edges | new_names.values())
1411            # Just like simple calculus, we need the derivative of the outside times the derivative of the inside
1412            outside = Function(
1413                self.signature.derivative(i, connection_names),
1414                self.inputs,
1415                self.shape_out | {connection_names[e]: t.shape[e] for e in input_edges},
1416            )
1417            assert outside.edges == self.edges | connection_names.values()
1418
1419            # The the derivative of the inner function
1420            # We rename the (former) input edges to the connection edges, but keep the remaining edges
1421            # (which are part of self.edges) untouched.
1422            inner = Derivative(t.rename(**connection_names), x, new_names)
1423
1424            # The two parts are then multiplied together on the connection names,
1425            # while broadcasted on their remaining shared edges.
1426            import tensorgrad.functions as F  # Import here to avoid circular import
1427
1428            part = F.sum(outside * inner, connection_names.values())
1429            parts.append(part)
1430        return Sum(parts)
1431
1432    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1433        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1434        G.add_node(0, name=(type(self).__name__, self.signature.name), tensor=self)
1435        edges = {}
1436        # (1) We add a node for the "function" tensor itself
1437        # TODO: Why is this needed?
1438        G.add_node(1, name=("f", self.signature.name), tensor=self)
1439        G.add_edge(0, 1)
1440        # (2) We add a node for each output edge
1441        # TODO: We should group out-edges by the size-type, similarly to Variable/Constant
1442        # And of course when we add symmetries to outputs, we need to add that too.
1443        for i, e in enumerate(self.shape_out):
1444            G.add_node(i + 2, name=("Edge Out", e))
1445            G.add_edge(1, i + 2)
1446            edges[e] = i + 2
1447        # (3) And we add nodes for all the input tensors
1448        for i, (t, input_edges) in enumerate(zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs)):
1449            # Compute graph from input tensor, and ensure it uses distinct node numbers
1450            # Tag the inputs with {i} since order matters for function inputs.
1451            G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, t, root_edge_label=f"{i}")
1452            # Connect tensor to function. We don't need to label here, since
1453            # the input tensor already has labeled its own edges as much as it needs to.
1454            for e in input_edges:
1455                G.add_edge(t_edges[e], 1)
1456            # Finally register free edges
1457            for e in t.edges:
1458                if e not in input_edges:
1459                    edges[e] = t_edges[e]
1460        return G, edges
1461
1462    def __repr__(self) -> str:
1463        args = []
1464        args.append(repr(self.signature))
1465        args.append(f"inputs=[{', '.join(map(repr, self.inputs))}]")
1466        args.append(f"shape_out={self.shape_out}")
1467        return f"Function({', '.join(args)})"
1468
1469    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1470        return any(t.depends_on(x) for t in self.inputs)
1471
1472    def _substitute(self, x: "Variable", y: "Tensor", memo: dict) -> "Tensor":
1473        inputs = [_substitute_memo(t, x, y, memo) for t in self.inputs]
1474        if all(a is b for a, b in zip(inputs, self.inputs)):
1475            return self
1476        return Function(self.signature, inputs, self.shape_out)

A tensor that represents a function applied to one or more input tensors.

Attributes: signature: The FunctionSignature for this function. inputs: A list of input tensors. shape_out: A dictionary mapping output edge names to sizes.

Function( signature: FunctionSignature, inputs: Iterable[Tensor], shape_out: dict[str, sympy.core.symbol.Symbol]) 
1285    def __init__(
1286        self,
1287        signature: FunctionSignature,
1288        inputs: Iterable[Tensor],
1289        shape_out: dict[str, Symbol],
1290    ):
1291        """
1292        A function tensor that takes one or more input tensors and produces an output tensor.
1293
1294        Args:
1295            signature: The FunctionSignature defining this function, or a string giving the function name.
1296            inputs: The input tensors and their input edge names. [(t0, e00, e01, ...), (t1, e10, ...), ...]
1297            shape_out: The names of the output edges of this function.
1298        """
1299        self.shape_out = self._check_shape([], shape_out)
1300        self.inputs = list(inputs)
1301        self.signature = signature
1302
1303        # Validation
1304        if self.shape_out.keys() != signature.edges:
1305            raise ValueError(f"Signature doesn't match shape: {self.shape_out.keys()=} != {signature.edges}")
1306        if len(self.inputs) != len(signature.inputs):
1307            raise ValueError(f"Expected {len(signature.inputs)} inputs, got {len(self.inputs)}")
1308        for t, es in zip(self.inputs, signature.inputs):
1309            if es - t.edges:
1310                raise ValueError(f"Input edges {es} not subset of {t.edges=}")
1311
1312        # Build final shape = union of output edges + broadcast edges from each input
1313        # Note that this approach pushes all the broadcasted edges to the end of the shape.
1314        # But we don't care about the order of the edges, so it's fine.
1315        self._shape = dict(self.shape_out)
1316        # We allow "broadcasting" for edges that are not in fn_sig.inputs[i].
1317        # So if an input has edges that are not used, they're broadcasted.
1318        for t, es in zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs):
1319            # any edges in t.edges - es are "broadcast"
1320            broadcast_edges = t.edges - es
1321            if broadcast_edges & self._shape.keys():
1322                raise ValueError("Broadcast edges must not be shared between inputs")
1323            for b in broadcast_edges:
1324                self._shape[b] = t.shape[b]

A function tensor that takes one or more input tensors and produces an output tensor.

Args: signature: The FunctionSignature defining this function, or a string giving the function name. inputs: The input tensors and their input edge names. [(t0, e00, e01, ...), (t1, e10, ...), ...] shape_out: The names of the output edges of this function.

shape_out
inputs
signature
def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
1432    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1433        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1434        G.add_node(0, name=(type(self).__name__, self.signature.name), tensor=self)
1435        edges = {}
1436        # (1) We add a node for the "function" tensor itself
1437        # TODO: Why is this needed?
1438        G.add_node(1, name=("f", self.signature.name), tensor=self)
1439        G.add_edge(0, 1)
1440        # (2) We add a node for each output edge
1441        # TODO: We should group out-edges by the size-type, similarly to Variable/Constant
1442        # And of course when we add symmetries to outputs, we need to add that too.
1443        for i, e in enumerate(self.shape_out):
1444            G.add_node(i + 2, name=("Edge Out", e))
1445            G.add_edge(1, i + 2)
1446            edges[e] = i + 2
1447        # (3) And we add nodes for all the input tensors
1448        for i, (t, input_edges) in enumerate(zip(self.inputs, self.signature.inputs)):
1449            # Compute graph from input tensor, and ensure it uses distinct node numbers
1450            # Tag the inputs with {i} since order matters for function inputs.
1451            G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, t, root_edge_label=f"{i}")
1452            # Connect tensor to function. We don't need to label here, since
1453            # the input tensor already has labeled its own edges as much as it needs to.
1454            for e in input_edges:
1455                G.add_edge(t_edges[e], 1)
1456            # Finally register free edges
1457            for e in t.edges:
1458                if e not in input_edges:
1459                    edges[e] = t_edges[e]
1460        return G, edges

Create a graph representation of the tensor, which can be used for isomorphism testing.

Returns: A tuple with the following values: - A NetworkX directed graph. - Each node in the top tree is labeled with the producing tensor. - Use name=hashable to label vertices - "edges", a dict of edge_name -> node id - Node 0 should be the root

def depends_on(self, x: Variable) -> bool: 
1469    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1470        return any(t.depends_on(x) for t in self.inputs)

Check if this tensor depends on the variable x.

Inherited Members
Tensor

class Derivative(Tensor): 
1479class Derivative(Tensor):
1480    """
1481    A tensor representing the derivative of another tensor with respect to a variable.
1482    """
1483
1484    def __init__(self, tensor: Tensor, wrt: Variable, new_names: Optional[dict[str, str]] = None):
1485        """
1486        A tensor representing the derivative of another tensor.
1487
1488        Args:
1489            tensor: The tensor to differentiate.
1490            x: The variable with respect to which to differentiate.
1491            new_names: A mapping for renaming edges (if not provided, it will be generated).
1492        """
1493        self.tensor = tensor
1494        self.x = wrt
1495        # If no edges were given, pick some names based on x, but avoid clashes with tensor.
1496        self.new_names = tensor._check_grad(wrt, new_names)
1497        self._shape = tensor.shape | {self.new_names[e]: wrt.shape[e] for e in wrt.edges}
1498
1499    def _simplify(self, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1500        if not self.tensor.depends_on(self.x):
1501            return Zero(**self.shape)
1502        inner = self.tensor.simplify(args)
1503        if args["grad_steps"] == 0:
1504            # If grad_steps is 0, we pass the simplify through the derivative.
1505            res = Derivative(inner, self.x, self.new_names)
1506        else:
1507            args["grad_steps"] -= 1
1508            # Have to call simplify twice to avoid an infinite loop when stacking multiple derivatives.
1509            grad = inner.grad(self.x, self.new_names)
1510            _record_simplify_provenance(args, inner, grad)
1511            res = grad.simplify(args)
1512        return res
1513
1514    def _grad(self, x: Variable, new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
1515        # To avoid an infinite loop, we let the grad pass through us, rather than creating a double derivative.
1516        return Derivative(self.tensor.grad(x, new_names), self.x, self.new_names)
1517
1518    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
1519        # The free edges of Derivative are both the free edges of self.tensor and the new_names.
1520        # This is the only place where we need to rename the "internal edges" of the tensor.
1521        return Derivative(
1522            self.tensor.rename(**kwargs),
1523            self.x,
1524            {o: kwargs.get(e, e) for o, e in self.new_names.items()},
1525        )
1526
1527    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1528        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1529        G.add_node(0, name="Derivative", tensor=self)
1530        edges = {}
1531        # We add a node for the "wrt" tensor
1532        # and connect new_edges to point to the respective edges in self.x
1533        G, x_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, self.x, root_edge_label="self.x")
1534        edges |= {e: x_edges[oe] for oe, e in self.new_names.items()}
1535        # Then we add the differentiated tensor
1536        G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, self.tensor, root_edge_label="self.tensor")
1537        edges |= t_edges
1538        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1539        return G, edges
1540
1541    def __repr__(self) -> str:
1542        return f"Derivative({self.tensor}, {self.x}, {self.new_names})"
1543
1544    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1545        return self.tensor.depends_on(x)

A tensor representing the derivative of another tensor with respect to a variable.

Derivative( tensor: Tensor, wrt: Variable, new_names: Optional[dict[str, str]] = None) 
1484    def __init__(self, tensor: Tensor, wrt: Variable, new_names: Optional[dict[str, str]] = None):
1485        """
1486        A tensor representing the derivative of another tensor.
1487
1488        Args:
1489            tensor: The tensor to differentiate.
1490            x: The variable with respect to which to differentiate.
1491            new_names: A mapping for renaming edges (if not provided, it will be generated).
1492        """
1493        self.tensor = tensor
1494        self.x = wrt
1495        # If no edges were given, pick some names based on x, but avoid clashes with tensor.
1496        self.new_names = tensor._check_grad(wrt, new_names)
1497        self._shape = tensor.shape | {self.new_names[e]: wrt.shape[e] for e in wrt.edges}

A tensor representing the derivative of another tensor.

Args: tensor: The tensor to differentiate. x: The variable with respect to which to differentiate. new_names: A mapping for renaming edges (if not provided, it will be generated).

tensor
x
new_names
def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
1527    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1528        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1529        G.add_node(0, name="Derivative", tensor=self)
1530        edges = {}
1531        # We add a node for the "wrt" tensor
1532        # and connect new_edges to point to the respective edges in self.x
1533        G, x_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, self.x, root_edge_label="self.x")
1534        edges |= {e: x_edges[oe] for oe, e in self.new_names.items()}
1535        # Then we add the differentiated tensor
1536        G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, self.tensor, root_edge_label="self.tensor")
1537        edges |= t_edges
1538        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1539        return G, edges

1545        return self.tensor.depends_on(x)

class Product(Tensor): 
1553class Product(Tensor):
1554    """
1555    A tensor representing the product (contraction) of several tensors.
1556    """
1557
1558    def __init__(self, factors: Iterable[Tensor]):
1559        """
1560        Initialize a Product tensor.
1561
1562        Args:
1563            factors: An iterable of tensors to be multiplied/contracted.
1564        """
1565        self.factors = list(factors)
1566        self._shape = {}
1567        for edge, ts in _group_edges(self.factors).items():
1568            if len(ts) == 1:
1569                self._shape[edge] = ts[0].shape[edge]
1570            elif len(ts) == 2:
1571                s1, s2 = [t.shape[edge] for t in ts]
1572                if s1 != s2:
1573                    raise ValueError(f"Edge {edge} has different sizes, {s1} != {s2}")
1574            else:
1575                raise ValueError(
1576                    f"edge {edge} had multiplicity {len(ts)}. Use an identity tensor to combine multiple edges."
1577                )
1578
1579    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
1580        # Rename the inner edges (contractions) to avoid the new names introduced
1581        # by the renaming of the free edges.
1582        new_names = {kwargs.get(e, e) for e in self.edges}
1583        contractions = {e for t in self.factors for e in t.edges} - self.edges
1584        # Note the `unused_edge_names` function avoids introducing new clashes
1585        # between the edges being renamed.
1586        rename = _unused_edge_names(contractions, new_names)
1587        # It's safe to add the kwargs to rename, since self._check_rename restricts kwargs to only
1588        # contain keys that are in self.edges.
1589        rename |= kwargs
1590        return Product([t.rename(**rename) for t in self.factors])
1591
1592    @staticmethod
1593    def merge(products: list["Product"]) -> "Product":
1594        """
1595        Merge several Product tensors into one, renaming inner edges so they are distinct.
1596
1597        Args:
1598            products: A list of Product tensors.
1599
1600        Returns:
1601            A new Product tensor merging the inputs.
1602        """
1603        used_edges = {e for p in products for e in p.edges}
1604        res = []
1605        for p in products:
1606            # Maybe this could also be expressed in terms of avoid_internal_edges
1607            inner_edges = {e for t in p.factors for e in t.edges if e not in p.edges}
1608            rename = _unused_edge_names(inner_edges, used_edges)
1609            for t in p.factors:
1610                res.append(t.rename(**rename))
1611            used_edges.update(rename.values())  # Later renames should not clash with this one
1612        return Product(res)
1613
1614    def _grad(self, x: Variable, new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
1615        # Since we are adding new edges to an internal tensor in the product, we need to make sure
1616        # none of the other tensors in the product have edges that clash with these new edges.
1617        inner_names = {e for t in self.factors for e in t.edges if e not in self.edges}
1618        new_edges = set(new_names.values()) | self.edges
1619        rename = _unused_edge_names(inner_names, new_edges)
1620        new_prod = Product([t.rename(**rename) for t in self.factors])
1621        assert new_prod.shape == self.shape, "Renaming should not change the product"
1622
1623        # The classic product rule of Calculus: d/dx (f * g) = f' * g + f * g'
1624        return Sum(
1625            [
1626                Product(new_prod.factors[:i] + [Derivative(t, x, new_names)] + new_prod.factors[i + 1 :])
1627                for i, t in enumerate(new_prod.factors)
1628            ]
1629        )
1630
1631    def _substitute(self, x: "Variable", y: "Tensor", memo: dict) -> "Tensor":
1632        factors = [_substitute_memo(t, x, y, memo) for t in self.factors]
1633        if all(a is b for a, b in zip(factors, self.factors)):
1634            return self
1635        return Product(factors)
1636
1637    def __repr__(self) -> str:
1638        if len(self.factors) <= 1:
1639            return f"Product({self.factors})"
1640        else:
1641            inner = "    " + ",\n    ".join(map(repr, self.factors)) + ","
1642            return f"Product([\n{inner}\n])"
1643
1644    def _simplify(self, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1645        tensors = [t.simplify(args=args) for t in self.factors]
1646
1647        # If any tensor in a product is 0, so is the whole product
1648        if any(isinstance(t, Zero) for t in tensors):
1649            return Zero(**self.shape)
1650
1651        # Decide whether to push products through sums. This can be useful to simplify networks for
1652        # presentation, but it can also blow up the number of terms. So we make it optional.
1653        if args.get("distributed_products", False):
1654            raise NotImplementedError("Not implemented yet")
1655
1656        # Combine nested products. Note that not combining products may be useful to keep the
1657        # "natural" contraction order. This can speed up evaluation, since sub-tensors can be reused.
1658        if args["associative_products"]:
1659            sub_products = [t if isinstance(t, Product) else Product([t]) for t in tensors]
1660            tensors = Product.merge(sub_products).factors
1661
1662        # We can do a "small" kind of distributed products, which is handling children that are single sums
1663        # Also, if a child is a sum with a single element, we can pull the weight up.
1664        # In general, we can pull out the least common multiple of the weights of the children.
1665        if single_sums := [t for t in tensors if isinstance(t, Sum) and len(t.terms) == 1]:
1666            tensors = [t if t not in single_sums else t.terms[0] for t in tensors]
1667            res_weight = math.prod(t.weights[0] for t in single_sums)
1668        else:
1669            res_weight = 1
1670
1671        def verify_edges(ts: list[Tensor], msg: str = "") -> None:
1672            cnt = Counter(e for t in ts for e in t.edges)
1673            assert not cnt or cnt.most_common()[0][1] <= 2, msg
1674
1675        # Simplify Delta Tensors
1676        verify_edges(tensors)
1677        tensors = Delta.simplify_outer(tensors)
1678        verify_edges(tensors)
1679
1680        # Combine / Cancel Product Functions
1681        if args["combine_products"]:
1682            from tensorgrad.functions import _PowerFunction
1683
1684            verify_edges(tensors)
1685            before = tensors
1686            tensors = _PowerFunction.simplify_outer(tensors, args)
1687            verify_edges(tensors, f"{before} -> {tensors}")
1688
1689        # Deterministic factor order: sort by the seed-stable structural
1690        # fingerprint, so commutative construction order (and PYTHONHASHSEED)
1691        # doesn't leak into the simplified result.
1692        if (canon := _get_canon()) is not None:
1693            tensors.sort(key=canon.refined_sort_key)
1694
1695        # Base cases
1696        if len(tensors) == 1:
1697            res = tensors[0]
1698        elif args["expand"]:
1699            terms = [[]]
1700            weights = [1]
1701            for t in tensors:
1702                # A (lazy) Rename wrapper can hide a Sum factor from the
1703                # distribution below; push the rename through eagerly, but
1704                # only here where expand needs to look through it.
1705                while isinstance(t, Rename) and isinstance(t.tensor, Sum):
1706                    t = t.tensor._rename(**t.mapping)
1707                if isinstance(t, Sum):
1708                    # Create cartesian product
1709                    terms = [term + [t0] for term in terms for t0 in t.terms]
1710                    weights = [w * w0 for w in weights for w0 in t.weights]
1711                else:
1712                    for term in terms:
1713                        term.append(t)
1714            # Recurse with expand=False to avoid infinite descent, but keep
1715            # all other caller flags intact.
1716            res = Sum([Product(ts) for ts in terms], weights).simplify(args={**args, "expand": False})
1717        else:
1718            res = Product(tensors)
1719
1720        if res_weight != 1:
1721            res = res_weight * res
1722
1723        return res
1724
1725    def components(self) -> list["Product"]:
1726        """
1727        Decompose the product into disjoint components (subgraphs).
1728
1729        Returns:
1730            A list of Product tensors representing disjoint components.
1731        """
1732        # Create graph of tensor connections
1733        G = nx.Graph()
1734        G.add_nodes_from(range(len(self.factors)))
1735        for i, t1 in enumerate(self.factors):
1736            for j, t2 in enumerate(self.factors[i + 1 :], i + 1):
1737                if set(t1.edges) & set(t2.edges):
1738                    G.add_edge(i, j)
1739
1740        # Find connected components
1741        component_sets = list(nx.connected_components(G))
1742        components = [Product([self.factors[i] for i in comp]) for comp in component_sets]
1743        assert Product(components).edges == self.edges
1744        return components
1745
1746    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1747        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1748        G.add_node(0, name=self.__class__.__name__, tensor=self)
1749        edges = {}
1750        inner_edges = defaultdict(list)
1751        for t in self.factors:
1752            G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, t)
1753            for e, node in t_edges.items():
1754                inner_edges[e].append(node)
1755        for e, nodes in inner_edges.items():
1756            if len(nodes) == 2:
1757                # Note that inner edges are bidirectional, since tensor products
1758                # are associative.
1759                n1, n2 = nodes
1760                G.add_edge(n1, n2)
1761                G.add_edge(n2, n1)
1762            else:
1763                # If the edge is only present once, it's an outer edge
1764                assert e in self.edges, f"{e} not in {self.edges}"
1765                (n1,) = nodes
1766                edges[e] = n1
1767        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1768        return G, edges
1769
1770    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1771        return any(t.depends_on(x) for t in self.factors)
1772
1773    def _to_einsum_eq(self) -> tuple[list[Tensor], str]:
1774        """
1775        Convert the product to an einsum equation.
1776
1777        The main utility is that Delta tensors are mostly removed in favor of hyper edges, which are more efficient to compute.
1778        However, some Deltas are still needed, so we return the list of tensors that should be
1779        used with the einsum equation.
1780
1781        Returns:
1782            A tuple (factors, equation) where factors is a list of tensors and
1783            equation is the corresponding einsum string.
1784
1785        Raises:
1786            ValueError: If there are too many unique edges.
1787        """
1788        unique_edges = {e for t in self.factors for e in t.edges}
1789        if (n := len(unique_edges)) > 52:
1790            raise ValueError(f"Too many unique edges ({n} > 52) to convert to einsum equation")
1791        name_to_idx = defaultdict(lambda: ascii_letters[len(name_to_idx)])
1792
1793        # The main challenge is to convert Delta tensors to hyper edges
1794        factors = []
1795        for ft in self.factors:
1796            if isinstance(ft, Delta) and ft.order >= 1:
1797                output_edges = ft.edges & self.edges
1798                input_edges = ft.edges - output_edges
1799                # However, einsum doesn't support duplicated indices in the output, like i->ii,
1800                # So we need to recreate some Delta tensors manually
1801                if len(input_edges) >= 1:
1802                    e0 = list(input_edges)[0]
1803                    if len(output_edges) <= 1:
1804                        for e in ft.edges:
1805                            name_to_idx[e] = name_to_idx[e0]
1806                    if len(output_edges) > 1:
1807                        for e in input_edges:
1808                            name_to_idx[e] = name_to_idx[e0]
1809                        compressed_delta = Delta(ft._size, e0, *output_edges)
1810                        factors.append(compressed_delta)
1811                else:
1812                    factors.append(ft)
1813            else:
1814                factors.append(ft)
1815
1816        equation = ",".join("".join(name_to_idx[e] for e in ft.edges) for ft in factors)
1817        equation += "->" + "".join(name_to_idx[e] for e in self.edges)
1818        return factors, equation

A tensor representing the product (contraction) of several tensors.

Product(factors: Iterable[Tensor])

1563            factors: An iterable of tensors to be multiplied/contracted.
1564        """
1565        self.factors = list(factors)
1566        self._shape = {}
1567        for edge, ts in _group_edges(self.factors).items():
1568            if len(ts) == 1:
1569                self._shape[edge] = ts[0].shape[edge]
1570            elif len(ts) == 2:
1571                s1, s2 = [t.shape[edge] for t in ts]
1572                if s1 != s2:
1573                    raise ValueError(f"Edge {edge} has different sizes, {s1} != {s2}")
1574            else:
1575                raise ValueError(
1576                    f"edge {edge} had multiplicity {len(ts)}. Use an identity tensor to combine multiple edges."
1577                )

@staticmethod
def merge(products: list[Product]) -> Product: 
1592    @staticmethod
1593    def merge(products: list["Product"]) -> "Product":
1594        """
1595        Merge several Product tensors into one, renaming inner edges so they are distinct.
1596
1597        Args:
1598            products: A list of Product tensors.
1599
1600        Returns:
1601            A new Product tensor merging the inputs.
1602        """
1603        used_edges = {e for p in products for e in p.edges}
1604        res = []
1605        for p in products:
1606            # Maybe this could also be expressed in terms of avoid_internal_edges
1607            inner_edges = {e for t in p.factors for e in t.edges if e not in p.edges}
1608            rename = _unused_edge_names(inner_edges, used_edges)
1609            for t in p.factors:
1610                res.append(t.rename(**rename))
1611            used_edges.update(rename.values())  # Later renames should not clash with this one
1612        return Product(res)

1725    def components(self) -> list["Product"]:
1726        """

1730            A list of Product tensors representing disjoint components.
1731        """

1742        components = [Product([self.factors[i] for i in comp]) for comp in component_sets]

1744        return components

def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
1746    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:

1749        edges = {}

1754                inner_edges[e].append(node)
1755        for e, nodes in inner_edges.items():
1756            if len(nodes) == 2:
1757                # Note that inner edges are bidirectional, since tensor products
1758                # are associative.
1759                n1, n2 = nodes
1760                G.add_edge(n1, n2)
1761                G.add_edge(n2, n1)
1762            else:
1763                # If the edge is only present once, it's an outer edge
1764                assert e in self.edges, f"{e} not in {self.edges}"
1765                (n1,) = nodes
1766                edges[e] = n1
1767        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1768        return G, edges

class Sum(Tensor): 
1826class Sum(Tensor):
1827    """
1828    A weighted sum of several tensors.
1829    """
1830
1831    def __init__(self, terms: Iterable[Tensor], weights: Optional[Iterable[Number]] = None):
1832        """
1833        A weighted sum of multiple tensors.
1834
1835        Args:
1836            terms: The tensors to add together.
1837            weights: The weights of each tensor in the sum. If not provided, all weights are 1.
1838        """
1839        terms = list(terms)
1840
1841        # Broadcasting means we always upgrade to the super set of edges
1842        self._shape = {}
1843        for e, ts in _group_edges(terms).items():
1844            s = ts[0].shape[e]
1845            if not all(t.shape[e] == s for t in ts):
1846                raise ValueError(f"Edge {e} had different sizes in tensors: {ts}")
1847            self._shape[e] = s
1848
1849        # Any tensors that lacks an edge will be broadcasted to have that edge.
1850        # (Iterate in shape order, not set order, for deterministic results.)
1851        self.terms = []
1852        for t in terms:
1853            missing = {e: s for e, s in self._shape.items() if e not in t.edges}
1854            # Note: don't broadcast if the tensor is already full, since that would create new
1855            # Ones([]) objects after simplification is supposed to have completed.
1856            self.terms.append(t @ Ones(**missing) if missing else t)
1857
1858        self.weights = [1] * len(terms) if weights is None else list(weights)
1859        assert len(terms) == len(self.weights)
1860
1861    def _rename(self, **kwargs: str) -> Tensor:
1862        return Sum([t.rename(**kwargs) for t in self.terms], self.weights)
1863
1864    def _grad(self, x: Variable, new_names: dict[str, str]) -> Tensor:
1865        return Sum([Derivative(t, x, new_names) for t in self.terms], self.weights)
1866
1867    def _substitute(self, x: "Variable", y: "Tensor", memo: dict) -> "Tensor":
1868        terms = [_substitute_memo(t, x, y, memo) for t in self.terms]
1869        if all(a is b for a, b in zip(terms, self.terms)):
1870            return self
1871        return Sum(terms, self.weights)
1872
1873    def _simplify(self, args: dict[str, Any]) -> Tensor:
1874        terms = [t.simplify(args=args) for t in self.terms]
1875
1876        term_counter = Counter()
1877        for w, t in zip(self.weights, terms):
1878            if args["associative_sums"] and isinstance(t, Sum):
1879                for w1, t1 in zip(t.weights, t.terms):
1880                    term_counter[_MatchEdgesKey(t1)] += w * w1
1881            else:
1882                term_counter[_MatchEdgesKey(t)] += w
1883
1884        if args["sum_combine_terms"]:
1885            # Identify tensors with multiplicity and combine them. We use tensor.canon_with_edges to identify tensors.
1886            # It is important that isomorphic tensors with different outer edge labels don't get matched. For example
1887            # (o-i o-<jk) is isomorphic with (o-j o-<ik), but they shouldn't be combined. This example comes up in the
1888            # Hessian of softmax.
1889            ws_tensors = [
1890                (w, key.value)
1891                for key, w in term_counter.items()
1892                if w != 0 and not isinstance(key.value, Zero)
1893            ]
1894        else:
1895            ws_tensors = [(w, key.value) for key, w in term_counter.items()]
1896
1897        # Remove zero tensors or zero weights.
1898        # Note: This won't change the shape of the tensor, since all summands have been broadcasted.
1899        ws_tensors = [(w, t) for w, t in ws_tensors if w != 0 and not isinstance(t, Zero)]
1900        # Base case. Here we can't just return Zero([]), since that would change the signature of the tensor.
1901        if not ws_tensors:
1902            return Zero(**self.shape)
1903        # Deterministic term order: sort by the seed-stable (name-sensitive)
1904        # structural fingerprint, so commutative construction order (and
1905        # PYTHONHASHSEED) doesn't leak into the simplified result. Positive
1906        # weights sort first purely for presentation (avoid a leading minus).
1907        if (canon := _get_canon()) is not None:
1908            ws_tensors.sort(key=lambda wt: (wt[0] < 0, canon.refined_sort_key(wt[1]), str(wt[0])))
1909        weights, tensors = zip(*ws_tensors)
1910        # If there is just one tensor with weight 1, we don't need LinearComb
1911        if weights == (1,):
1912            return tensors[0]
1913        return Sum(tensors, weights)
1914
1915    def __repr__(self) -> str:
1916        return f"Sum({self.terms}, {self.weights})"
1917
1918    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1919        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1920        G.add_node(0, name=self.__class__.__name__, tensor=self)
1921        edges = {}
1922        # Create special "Plus nodes" to collect common edges
1923        for i, e in enumerate(self.edges):
1924            # Note: No need to add the shape here. It's already in the sub-tensors
1925            G.add_node(i + 1, name="Plus Node")
1926            edges[e] = i + 1
1927        for t, w in zip(self.terms, self.weights):
1928            # The weights are a little akward. There a lot of options for how to handle them.
1929            # E.g. the idea of just using a weighted Delta([]) somehow. But this works.
1930            G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, t, root_edge_label=f"Weight {w}")
1931            # Connect tensor edges to the plus nodes
1932            for e, node in t_edges.items():
1933                G.add_edge(node, edges[e])
1934        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1935        return G, edges
1936
1937    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1938        return any(t.depends_on(x) for t in self.terms)

A weighted sum of several tensors.

Sum( terms: Iterable[Tensor], weights: Optional[Iterable[numbers.Number]] = None) 
1831    def __init__(self, terms: Iterable[Tensor], weights: Optional[Iterable[Number]] = None):
1832        """
1833        A weighted sum of multiple tensors.
1834
1835        Args:
1836            terms: The tensors to add together.
1837            weights: The weights of each tensor in the sum. If not provided, all weights are 1.
1838        """
1839        terms = list(terms)
1840
1841        # Broadcasting means we always upgrade to the super set of edges
1842        self._shape = {}
1843        for e, ts in _group_edges(terms).items():
1844            s = ts[0].shape[e]
1845            if not all(t.shape[e] == s for t in ts):
1846                raise ValueError(f"Edge {e} had different sizes in tensors: {ts}")
1847            self._shape[e] = s
1848
1849        # Any tensors that lacks an edge will be broadcasted to have that edge.
1850        # (Iterate in shape order, not set order, for deterministic results.)
1851        self.terms = []
1852        for t in terms:
1853            missing = {e: s for e, s in self._shape.items() if e not in t.edges}
1854            # Note: don't broadcast if the tensor is already full, since that would create new
1855            # Ones([]) objects after simplification is supposed to have completed.
1856            self.terms.append(t @ Ones(**missing) if missing else t)
1857
1858        self.weights = [1] * len(terms) if weights is None else list(weights)
1859        assert len(terms) == len(self.weights)

A weighted sum of multiple tensors.

Args: terms: The tensors to add together. weights: The weights of each tensor in the sum. If not provided, all weights are 1.

terms
weights
def structural_graph( self) -> tuple[networkx.classes.multidigraph.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]: 
1918    def structural_graph(self) -> tuple[nx.MultiDiGraph, dict[str, int]]:
1919        G = nx.MultiDiGraph()
1920        G.add_node(0, name=self.__class__.__name__, tensor=self)
1921        edges = {}
1922        # Create special "Plus nodes" to collect common edges
1923        for i, e in enumerate(self.edges):
1924            # Note: No need to add the shape here. It's already in the sub-tensors
1925            G.add_node(i + 1, name="Plus Node")
1926            edges[e] = i + 1
1927        for t, w in zip(self.terms, self.weights):
1928            # The weights are a little akward. There a lot of options for how to handle them.
1929            # E.g. the idea of just using a weighted Delta([]) somehow. But this works.
1930            G, t_edges = _add_structural_graph(G, t, root_edge_label=f"Weight {w}")
1931            # Connect tensor edges to the plus nodes
1932            for e, node in t_edges.items():
1933                G.add_edge(node, edges[e])
1934        assert edges.keys() == self.edges
1935        return G, edges

Create a graph representation of the tensor, which can be used for isomorphism testing.

Returns: A tuple with the following values: - A NetworkX directed graph. - Each node in the top tree is labeled with the producing tensor. - Use name=hashable to label vertices - "edges", a dict of edge_name -> node id - Node 0 should be the root

def depends_on(self, x: Variable) -> bool: 
1937    def depends_on(self, x: "Variable") -> bool:
1938        return any(t.depends_on(x) for t in self.terms)

Check if this tensor depends on the variable x.
